lessons.title: Electric Charge
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Rub a balloon on your hair and it sticks to a wall; pull off a wool sweater in the dark and you hear tiny crackles. Both are the same invisible thing at work: **electric charge**.

**Build.** All stuff is made of tiny particles called **atoms**. Atoms contain even smaller bits that carry **charge** — a basic property that makes them push or pull on each other. There are two kinds, named **positive (+)** and **negative (−)**. The simple rule: **like charges repel** (two positives, or two negatives, push apart) and **opposite charges attract** (a positive and a negative pull together).

Normally an object has equal positive and negative charge, so it is **neutral** (no net push or pull). When you rub two things together, some negative bits (called **electrons** — the lightweight negative particles) rub off one onto the other. Now one object has extra negative charge and the other has extra positive. This stored, non-moving charge is **static electricity**, and the attraction it makes is why the balloon sticks.

Materials differ in how easily charge moves through them. A **conductor** (like metal) lets charge flow freely. An **insulator** (like rubber or plastic) holds charge in place. Around any charge is an **electric field** — an invisible region where another charge would feel a push or pull. We draw it with arrows pointing away from + and toward −.

**Recap.** Charge comes in + and −; like repels, opposite attracts. Rubbing moves electrons to make static charge. Conductors let charge flow, insulators don’t, and an electric field is the reach of a charge’s push.

items.prompt: What happens when you bring two negatively charged objects close together?
choices: They repel (push apart), because like charges repel
They attract, because all charges attract
Nothing, because only positives can interact
They become neutral instantly
items.explanation: Two negatives are 'like' charges, so they push apart. Choice 2 forgets that opposite charges attract but like charges repel; choice 3 wrongly claims negatives do nothing; choice 4 invents instant neutralizing that doesn't happen just from being near.

items.prompt: A positive charge and a negative charge are placed near each other. What do they do?
choices: Attract — opposite charges pull together
Repel — they always push apart
Ignore each other entirely
Spin in circles forever with no net force
items.explanation: Opposite charges attract, so + and − pull toward each other. Choice 2 confuses the rule for like charges; choices 3 and 4 deny the real attractive force between them.

items.prompt: When you rub a balloon on your hair and it picks up negative charge, what actually moved?
choices: Electrons (tiny negative particles) rubbed from your hair onto the balloon
Positive particles flew off the balloon into the air
Whole atoms jumped from hair to balloon
Nothing moved; the balloon just got warmer
items.explanation: Rubbing transfers lightweight electrons; gaining extra electrons makes the balloon negative. Choice 2 misnames what moves — it's electrons, not positives leaving; choice 3 overstates it (only electrons move, not whole atoms); choice 4 ignores the charge transfer.

items.prompt: An object is described as 'neutral'. What does that mean?
choices: It has equal amounts of positive and negative charge, so no net charge
It has no atoms in it at all
It is made only of positive charge
It cannot ever become charged
items.explanation: Neutral means the positive and negative charges balance out to zero net charge. Choice 2 is impossible for ordinary matter; choice 3 describes a charged object; choice 4 is wrong because rubbing can charge a neutral object.

items.prompt: Which material is the best example of a conductor?
choices: A copper wire
A rubber band
A glass cup
A dry wooden stick
items.explanation: Metals like copper let charge flow freely, so copper is a conductor. Rubber, glass, and dry wood are insulators that hold charge in place rather than letting it move.

items.prompt: What is an 'electric field' around a charge?
choices: A region of space where another charge would feel a push or pull
A wire that carries charge between objects
The total number of electrons in an object
A metal box that blocks all charge
items.explanation: A field is the invisible reach of a charge — anywhere a second charge would feel force. Choice 2 describes a conductor, not a field; choice 3 confuses field with a count of charge; choice 4 describes shielding, not the field itself.

items.prompt: In a field diagram, electric field arrows point away from a positive charge and toward a negative charge. What do these arrows represent?
choices: The direction a small positive test charge would be pushed
The path electrons take inside a metal wire
The speed of light leaving the charge
The temperature of the charged object
items.explanation: By convention field arrows show the force direction on a positive test charge: away from + and toward −. Choice 2 is about current in wires, not fields; choices 3 and 4 are unrelated quantities.

items.prompt: You walk across a carpet and get a shock touching a doorknob. Why?
choices: You built up static charge, which jumped to the metal knob as a spark
The doorknob is a battery storing voltage
Your body generated new atoms that escaped
Heat from your hand ignited the air
items.explanation: Friction with the carpet transfers electrons, leaving you charged; the extra charge leaps to the conducting knob as a spark. Choice 2 wrongly treats the knob as a power source; choice 3 invents new matter; choice 4 misattributes it to heat.

items.prompt: Why does a neutral wall attract a charged balloon?
choices: The balloon's charge nudges the wall's charges, pulling opposite ones slightly closer (induced attraction)
The wall is also strongly charged the same way
Gravity from the balloon pulls the wall
Air pressure glues them together
items.explanation: A charged balloon rearranges the wall's own charges so the opposite ones sit nearer the balloon, creating a net attraction even though the wall is neutral overall. Choice 2 is wrong (the wall stays neutral); choices 3 and 4 misname the cause.

lessons.title: Current, Voltage, Resistance & Ohm's Law
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Flip a switch and a light comes on. Behind that simple act are three ideas that, once you picture them as water in pipes, become easy: **current**, **voltage**, and **resistance**.

**Build.** Imagine water flowing in a loop of pipes pushed by a pump.

**Current** is the *flow* — how much charge passes a point each second. It is measured in **amperes (amps, A)**. In a wire, current is moving charge, just as flow is moving water.

**Voltage** is the *push* — like the pressure the pump provides. More pressure shoves more water; more voltage pushes more charge. Voltage is measured in **volts (V)**. A battery is like a pump that supplies this push.

**Resistance** is how much the path *fights* the flow — like a narrow section of pipe. A narrow pipe lets less water through for the same pressure. Resistance is measured in **ohms (Ω, the Greek letter omega)**.

These three connect through **Ohm’s law**: $$V = I\\,R$$ — voltage equals current times resistance ($I$ is the symbol for current, $R$ for resistance). In words: for a fixed resistance, more voltage means more current; for a fixed voltage, more resistance means less current. Rearranged, $I = V/R$.

A common myth: that a bulb 'uses up' the current. It does not — the same amount of charge that flows in flows out. What the bulb uses up is **energy**, which the charge carries and the bulb turns into light and heat.

**Recap.** Voltage is the push (volts), current is the flow (amps), resistance is the squeeze (ohms), tied together by $V = I\\,R$. Bulbs use energy, not current.

items.prompt: In the water-pipe analogy, what does VOLTAGE correspond to?
choices: The water pressure pushing the flow
The amount of water flowing per second
How narrow the pipe is
The temperature of the water
items.explanation: Voltage is the push, just like pressure from a pump. Choice 2 is current (the flow rate); choice 3 is resistance (the narrowing); choice 4 has no role in the basic analogy.

items.prompt: What does electric CURRENT measure, and in what unit?
choices: How much charge flows past a point each second, in amperes (amps)
The push behind the charge, in volts
How hard the path fights flow, in ohms
The energy stored in a battery, in joules
items.explanation: Current is the rate of charge flow, measured in amps. Choice 2 describes voltage; choice 3 describes resistance; choice 4 describes stored energy, not current.

items.prompt: Resistance is to a circuit as ___ is to the water analogy.
choices: a narrow section of pipe that restricts flow
the pump that drives the water
the total volume of water in the loop
the color of the pipe
items.explanation: Resistance fights the flow like a narrow pipe restricts water. The pump is voltage (the push), total water relates to charge, and pipe color is irrelevant.

items.prompt: Ohm's law is written V = I R. What does it say in plain words?
choices: Voltage equals current times resistance
Voltage equals current divided by resistance
Current equals voltage times resistance
Resistance equals voltage times current
items.explanation: V = I R means voltage = current × resistance. Choice 2 wrongly divides; choice 3 rearranges incorrectly (it should be I = V/R); choice 4 confuses resistance with power (P = V I).

items.prompt: A 12-volt battery drives a current through a 4-ohm resistor. Using I = V/R, what is the current?
choices: 3 amps
48 amps
0.33 amps
16 amps
items.explanation: I = V/R = 12 ÷ 4 = 3 amps. Choice 2 multiplies instead of dividing (12×4); choice 3 inverts it (4÷12); choice 4 adds the numbers (12+4).

items.prompt: For a fixed resistance, what happens to the current if you double the voltage?
choices: The current doubles too
The current is halved
The current stays the same
The current drops to zero
items.explanation: Since I = V/R, doubling V (with R fixed) doubles I. Choice 2 reverses the relationship; choice 3 ignores Ohm's law; choice 4 is simply wrong.

items.prompt: A common misconception is that a light bulb 'uses up the current.' What is actually true?
choices: The current flowing out of the bulb equals the current flowing in; the bulb uses up ENERGY, not current
Half the current disappears inside the bulb
The bulb destroys the electrons that reach it
Current only flows on the way to the bulb, never back
items.explanation: Charge is conserved: the same current that enters the bulb leaves it. What the bulb consumes is energy, converted to light and heat. Choices 2 and 3 wrongly destroy charge; choice 4 forgets a circuit is a complete loop.

items.prompt: Why must a circuit form a complete loop for current to flow?
choices: Charge needs a continuous path back to the source, like water needs a closed loop to keep circulating
Because electrons are afraid of dead ends
Only complete loops can store voltage
A loop makes the wire colder
items.explanation: Charge flows only when there is an unbroken path from the source through the components and back; break the loop and flow stops, just like cutting a water loop stops circulation. The other choices are nonsense distractors.

items.prompt: Which set correctly pairs each quantity with its unit?
choices: Voltage – volts, Current – amps, Resistance – ohms
Voltage – amps, Current – ohms, Resistance – volts
Voltage – ohms, Current – volts, Resistance – amps
Voltage – watts, Current – volts, Resistance – amps
items.explanation: Voltage is in volts, current in amps, resistance in ohms. The other options scramble the units; watts (choice 4) measure power, not voltage.

items.prompt: If you keep the voltage the same but swap in a resistor with MORE resistance, the current will:
choices: Decrease, because a higher resistance fights the flow more (I = V/R)
Increase, because resistance helps push charge
Stay exactly the same
Become negative
items.explanation: With V fixed, raising R lowers I (I = V/R) — a narrower pipe lets less through. Choice 2 misunderstands resistance as a push; choice 3 ignores Ohm's law; choice 4 is impossible here.

lessons.title: Simple Circuits: Series, Parallel & Power
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Old holiday lights where one dead bulb killed the whole string — versus your home, where unplugging a lamp doesn’t shut off the fridge. The difference is **series** versus **parallel** wiring.

**Build.** A **series** circuit connects components one after another in a single loop, like beads on one string. The **same current** flows through every part, but the **voltage splits** among them. The catch: break any one part and the whole loop dies — that’s the old light string.

A **parallel** circuit gives each component its own branch off the main wires, like rungs of a ladder. Here **each branch gets the full voltage** of the source, and the current divides among the branches. Break one branch and the others keep working. This is why **home wiring is parallel**: every outlet receives the full voltage, and devices run independently.

Electricity does work, and the rate of doing it is **power** — energy used per second, measured in **watts (W)**. For circuits, power is voltage times current: $$P = V\\,I$$ — a higher push or a bigger flow means more power. A 100-watt bulb uses energy twice as fast as a 50-watt one.

Your electric bill, though, charges for total **energy**, not power. Energy = power × time. Run 1000 watts for 1 hour and you use one **kilowatt-hour (kWh)** — the standard billing unit. That’s why bills are in kWh: they measure how much energy you consumed over time, not the instantaneous rate.

**Recap.** Series shares current and splits voltage (one break stops all); parallel gives each branch full voltage and works independently (so homes use it). Power $P = V\\,I$ is energy per second in watts; bills use kWh, which is energy.

items.prompt: In a SERIES circuit, what is true about the current?
choices: The same current flows through every component
Each component gets its own separate current
Current only flows through the first component
There is no current in a series circuit
items.explanation: A series circuit is one single loop, so the same current passes through every part. Choice 2 describes parallel branches; choices 3 and 4 deny that the whole loop carries current.

items.prompt: Why does home wiring use PARALLEL connections?
choices: So each outlet gets the full voltage and devices work independently
So all devices must be on at the same time
Because parallel uses no electricity
So one broken bulb shuts off the whole house
items.explanation: In parallel each branch receives the full source voltage and one device failing doesn't cut the others. Choice 2 and 4 actually describe series behavior; choice 3 is false (parallel still uses energy).

items.prompt: Two identical bulbs are wired in PARALLEL across a battery. How much voltage does each bulb get?
choices: The full battery voltage — each branch sees the whole voltage
Half the battery voltage each
Zero, because they share
Double the battery voltage
items.explanation: In parallel every branch is connected directly across the source, so each bulb gets the full voltage. Choice 2 describes splitting in series; choices 3 and 4 are impossible.

items.prompt: In a string of bulbs wired in series, one bulb burns out. What happens?
choices: All the bulbs go dark, because the single loop is broken
Only the broken bulb goes dark; the rest stay lit
The other bulbs get brighter
Nothing changes at all
items.explanation: A series circuit is one loop; breaking it anywhere stops the whole current, so all bulbs go out. Choice 2 describes parallel behavior; choices 3 and 4 contradict the broken loop.

items.prompt: Electrical power is given by P = V I. What does power measure?
choices: The rate at which energy is used (energy per second), in watts
The total energy stored, in joules
The push of the source, in volts
The resistance of the wire, in ohms
items.explanation: Power is energy per second, measured in watts, and equals voltage times current. Choice 2 is total energy (not the rate); choice 3 is voltage; choice 4 is resistance.

items.prompt: A device runs at 120 volts and draws 2 amps. Using P = V I, what is its power?
choices: 240 watts
60 watts
122 watts
2 watts
items.explanation: P = V × I = 120 × 2 = 240 watts. Choice 2 divides (120÷2); choice 3 adds (120+2); choice 4 just repeats the current.

items.prompt: Why are electricity bills measured in kilowatt-hours (kWh)?
choices: A kWh measures total energy used (power × time), which is what you actually consume
Because a kWh measures voltage
Because a kWh is the same as a watt
Because bills only care about the instant a device is on
items.explanation: A kWh is energy: 1000 watts running for 1 hour. Bills charge for energy over time, not the instantaneous power. Choice 2 confuses energy with voltage; choice 3 is wrong (a kWh is energy, a watt is power); choice 4 ignores the role of time.

items.prompt: How much energy does a 1000-watt heater use if it runs for 1 hour?
choices: 1 kilowatt-hour (kWh)
1000 kilowatt-hours
1 watt
60 kilowatt-hours
items.explanation: 1000 watts = 1 kilowatt; running for 1 hour gives 1 kW × 1 h = 1 kWh. Choice 2 forgets to convert watts to kilowatts; choice 3 confuses energy with power; choice 4 wrongly multiplies by minutes.

items.prompt: In a SERIES circuit with two equal resistors, what happens to the source voltage?
choices: It splits, so each resistor gets a share of the total voltage
Each resistor gets the full source voltage
The voltage disappears entirely
The voltage doubles across each resistor
items.explanation: In series the same current flows but the voltage is divided among components. Choice 2 describes parallel; choices 3 and 4 are impossible.

items.prompt: Which everyday situation matches a PARALLEL circuit?
choices: Unplugging your toaster does not turn off the kitchen lights
Old holiday lights where one dead bulb kills the whole string
A flashlight that only works if every battery is present in one line
A single wire loop with one switch and one bulb
items.explanation: Parallel branches work independently, so removing one device leaves the others on — just like home wiring. Choices 2 and 3 describe series circuits (one break stops everything); choice 4 is a simple single loop.

lessons.title: Magnetism & Electromagnetism
lessons.content:
**Hook.** A fridge magnet, a compass that always finds north, and the motor spinning inside a fan are all the same physics — and, astonishingly, magnetism and electricity are two sides of one thing.

**Build.** A **magnet** is an object that pulls on certain metals (like iron). Every magnet has two ends called **poles**: a **north (N)** pole and a **south (S)** pole. The rule echoes charges: **like poles repel, opposite poles attract**. Around a magnet is a **magnetic field** — the region where its force acts — drawn as lines looping from N to S.

A key fact: you can never get a lone pole. Cut a bar magnet in half and you get two smaller magnets, each with its own N and S. There are no **magnetic monopoles** (single isolated poles) — unlike electric charge, which can be purely + or purely −.

The Earth itself is a giant magnet, so a **compass** (a tiny pivoting magnet) lines up with Earth’s magnetic field and points roughly north.

Now the deep link. **Electric current makes magnetism:** send current through a coil of wire and it behaves like a magnet — an **electromagnet**. Turn the current off and the magnetism vanishes, which is how junkyard cranes grab and drop cars. And the reverse holds: **a changing magnetic field makes current.** Spin a magnet near a coil and you push current through it.

These two effects power our world. A **motor** uses current in a magnetic field to make motion (fans, drills). A **generator** runs it backward — motion plus magnetism makes current (power plants).

**Recap.** Magnets have N and S poles (like repel, opposite attract); poles can’t be isolated. Current makes magnetism (electromagnets, motors) and changing magnetism makes current (generators).

items.prompt: What happens when you bring the NORTH pole of one magnet near the NORTH pole of another?
choices: They repel, because like poles repel
They attract, because all poles attract
Nothing happens between poles
One pole turns into a south pole
items.explanation: Like poles (N–N or S–S) push apart, mirroring like charges. Choice 2 forgets that only opposite poles attract; choice 3 denies the force; choice 4 invents a pole flip that doesn't occur.

items.prompt: You cut a bar magnet exactly in half. What do you get?
choices: Two smaller magnets, each with its own north and south pole
One piece that is only north and one that is only south
Two pieces with no magnetism at all
A single isolated north pole
items.explanation: Magnetic poles always come in pairs, so cutting a magnet yields two complete magnets. Choices 2 and 4 describe isolated poles (monopoles), which don't exist; choice 3 wrongly removes all magnetism.

items.prompt: Why can't you isolate a single magnetic pole (a monopole)?
choices: Magnetic poles always occur in N–S pairs; no isolated pole has ever been found
Because poles are made of electric charge
Because magnets are too small to cut
You can — every broken magnet gives one lone pole
items.explanation: Unlike electric charge (which can be purely + or −), magnetic poles always come paired N and S, so no monopole exists. Choice 2 conflates poles with charge; choice 3 is irrelevant; choice 4 directly contradicts the rule.

items.prompt: Why does a compass needle point roughly north?
choices: The Earth acts like a giant magnet, and the needle (a small magnet) aligns with Earth's magnetic field
The needle is attracted to the cold of the North Pole
Gravity pulls the needle northward
The Sun magnetizes the needle each morning
items.explanation: Earth's own magnetic field steers the free-pivoting compass magnet so it lines up north–south. Choice 2 confuses temperature with magnetism; choice 3 misattributes it to gravity; choice 4 is fictional.

items.prompt: What is an electromagnet?
choices: A coil of wire that becomes magnetic when electric current flows through it
A permanent magnet that never loses its magnetism
A magnet made by rubbing two iron bars
A type of battery that stores magnetism
items.explanation: Running current through a coil creates magnetism — an electromagnet — which switches off when the current stops. Choice 2 describes a permanent magnet; choice 3 is a different (weak) method; choice 4 misdescribes it as a battery.

items.prompt: A junkyard crane uses an electromagnet to lift cars and then drop them. How does it release the car?
choices: It switches off the current, so the magnetism disappears and the car drops
It reverses gravity under the car
It heats the car until it falls
It cuts the car in half
items.explanation: An electromagnet is magnetic only while current flows; cutting the current removes the magnetism and releases the load. The other choices are physically impossible or irrelevant.

items.prompt: Which statement captures the two-way link between electricity and magnetism?
choices: Electric current creates magnetism, and a changing magnetic field creates electric current
Electricity and magnetism are completely unrelated
Only magnets can make electricity; current never makes magnetism
Magnetism only exists inside batteries
items.explanation: This two-way relationship (current makes magnetism; changing magnetism makes current) underlies motors and generators. Choice 2 denies the link; choice 3 ignores half of it; choice 4 is false.

items.prompt: What is the basic idea behind an electric MOTOR?
choices: Current flowing in a magnetic field produces a force that creates motion
Motion alone with no electricity creates a spinning magnet
It turns light directly into heat
It stores charge like a battery
items.explanation: A motor uses current within a magnetic field to push parts and spin — turning electrical energy into motion. Choice 2 describes a generator's input, not a motor; choices 3 and 4 describe unrelated devices.

items.prompt: How does a GENERATOR at a power plant produce electricity?
choices: Motion spins magnets and coils relative to each other, and the changing magnetic field drives a current
It creates electrons out of nothing
It stores sunlight and releases it as current at night
It uses a battery to push current into the grid
items.explanation: A generator is a motor run backward: mechanical motion plus a changing magnetic field induces current. Choice 2 violates conservation of charge; choice 3 describes a different (solar) idea; choice 4 misdescribes a generator as a battery.

items.prompt: How does a magnetic field compare to an electric field in how we draw it?
choices: Magnetic field lines loop from a magnet's north pole around to its south pole
Magnetic field lines start and stop at a single isolated pole
Magnetic fields have no direction at all
Magnetic field lines always point straight up
items.explanation: We draw magnetic field lines running from N to S outside the magnet, forming closed loops — consistent with there being no isolated poles. Choice 2 implies a monopole; choices 3 and 4 misstate that fields have direction set by the poles.
